29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
51	
52	\section{The identity component of a group scheme}
53	\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}
54	
55	The identity component is an abstract feature of group schemes locally
56	of finite type over a field. We record the definition and its
57	open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
58	Lean substrate
59	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
60	outside the Picard context.
61	
62	\begin{definition}\leanok
63	[Identity component of a group scheme]
64	  \label{def:identity_component_group_scheme}
65	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
66	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps;
67	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
68	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2851-L2863).
69	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
70	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lem:agps}
71	  %  Let \(k\) be a field, and \(G\) a group scheme locally of finite
72	  % type.  Let \(G^0\) denote the connected component of the identity element \(e\).
73	  %
74	  % \tu{(1)} Then \(G\) is separated.
75	  %
76	  % \tu{(2)} Then \(G\) is smooth if it has a geometrically
77	  % reduced open subscheme.
78	  %
79	  %  \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
80	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
81	  % extending \(k\).
82	  %  \end{lem}"
83	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps.}
84	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type
85	  whose identity section \(e : \Spec k \to G\) factors through \(G\). The
86	  \emph{identity component} of \(G\), denoted \(G^0\), is the locally closed
87	  subscheme of \(G\) whose underlying topological space is the connected
88	  component of \(|G|\) containing the image of \(e\), equipped with the
89	  reduced-induced (a priori) subscheme structure inherited from \(G\).
90	  In characteristic zero, or more generally when \(G\) has a
91	  geometrically reduced open subscheme, the substructure agrees with
92	  the open subscheme structure of \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
93	\end{definition}
94

96	% into per-conclusion theorem blocks, each with its own \lean{...} pin to
97	% a Lean declaration. The verbatim source quote of lem:agps~(3) is
98	% reproduced once at \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} below
99	% (covering all four conclusions); the three subsequent blocks reference
100	% that quote rather than duplicating it. Each theorem carries its own
101	% \begin{proof} block (the matching paragraph of Kleiman's proof of
102	% lem:agps~(3)); the verbatim \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:} comment from
103	% Kleiman's proof, covering all four conclusions, precedes the proof of
104	% \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.

106	\begin{theorem}
107	[Identity component is a clopen subscheme]
108	  \label{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
109	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}
110	
111	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
112	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
113	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
114	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
115	  % " \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
116	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
117	  % extending \(k\)."
118	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3).}
119	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
120	  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\)
121	  (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is an open and closed
122	  subscheme of \(G\): the inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is both an
123	  open immersion and a closed immersion of schemes.
124	\end{theorem}
125	
126	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
127	% L2864-L2911, proof of lem:agps; we reproduce the part dealing with
128	% (3).)
129	% "Consider (3).  By definition, \(G^0\) is the largest connected subspace
130	% containing \(e\).  But the closure of a connected subspace is plainly
131	% connected; so \(G^0\) is closed.  By \cite[6.1.9]{EGAI}, in any locally
132	% Noetherian topological space, the connected components are open.  Thus
133	% \(G^0\) is open too.
134	%
135	% Since \(G^0\) is connected and has a \(k\)-point, \(G^0\) is geometrically
136	% connected by \cite[4.5.14]{EGAIV2}.  Thus forming \(G^0\) commutes with
137	% extending \(k\).
138	%
139	% Furthermore, \(G^0\x G^0\) is connected by \cite[4.5.8]{EGAIV2}.
140	% So \(\alpha(G^0\x G^0)\subset G\) is connected, and contains \(e\),
141	% so lies in \(G^0\).  Thus \(G^0\) is a subgroup.
142	%
143	% To prove \(G^0\) is geometrically irreducible and quasi-compact, we may
144	% replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure.  Then \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) is
145	% reduced by \cite[4.6.1]{EGAIV2}.  Hence \(\alpha\) induces a map from
146	% \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) into \(G^0_{\red}\).  So \(G^0_{\red}\) is a
147	% subgroup.  Thus we may replace \(G^0\) by \(G^0_{\red}\).
148	% [\dots]
149	% Hence \(G^0\) is irreducible locally at \(h\).  But \(h\) is
150	% arbitrary, and \(G^0\) is connected.  So \(G^0\) is irreducible by

152	% [\dots]
153	% Now, \(U\x U\) is affine, so quasi-compact.  Hence \(G^0\) is

155	% \(G^0\) is of finite type.  Thus (3) holds."
156	\begin{proof}
157	  \uses{def:identity_component_group_scheme}
158	  Following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~lem:agps~(3) (clopen-subscheme part).
159	  Since \(e\) is a \(k\)-rational point, the singleton \(\{e\}\) is a closed
160	  point of \(G\). The connected component \(G^0\) of \(|G|\) at \(e\) is the
161	  largest connected subspace containing \(e\); the closure of a connected
162	  subspace is connected, hence \(G^0\) is closed. On the other hand, a
163	  locally Noetherian topological space has open connected components
164	  (EGA I 6.1.9), so \(G^0 \subseteq |G|\) is also open. Equip \(G^0\) with
165	  the open-subscheme structure; the inclusion
166	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is then both an open and a closed immersion.

171	\leanok
172	[Identity component inclusion is a group-scheme homomorphism]
173	  \label{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}
174	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism}
175	
176	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
177	  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
178	  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
179	  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
180	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
181	  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
182	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
183	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
184	  type. The clopen inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\)
185	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) is a homomorphism of
186	  \(k\)-group schemes: \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure
187	  from \(G\), and the inclusion morphism in the over-category over

191	\begin{proof}
192	  \uses{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup, def:identity_component_group_scheme}
193	  Following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~lem:agps~(3) (subgroup part); the
194	  verbatim source quote is reproduced at
195	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
196	  The product \(G^0 \times_k G^0\) is connected
197	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.8). Let \(\alpha : G \times_k G \to G\),
198	  \(\alpha(g, h) := g h^{-1}\), be the algebraic-group structure map.
199	  Since \(\alpha(G^0 \times_k G^0)\) is connected and contains \(e\), its
200	  image lies in the clopen identity component \(G^0\)
201	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}). Hence \(G^0\) is closed
202	  under the group operation (and under inversion, by taking \(g = e\)):
203	  the inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is a homomorphism of
204	  \(k\)-group schemes, and \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme
205	  structure from \(G\).
206	\end{proof}

208	\begin{theorem}
209	
210	\leanok
211	[Identity component is of finite type and geometrically irreducible]
212	  \label{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
213	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible}
214	
215	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
216	  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
217	  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
218	  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).

220	  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
221	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
222	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
223	  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\) is of finite type over
224	  \(k\) --- equivalently, locally of finite type \emph{and}
225	  quasi-compact --- and geometrically irreducible: after base change
226	  to the algebraic closure \(\bar k\), the underlying scheme
227	  \(G^0_{\bar k}\) is an irreducible \(\bar k\)-scheme.
228	\end{theorem}
229	
230	\begin{proof}
231	  \uses{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup, thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes, def:identity_component_group_scheme}
232	  Following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~lem:agps~(3) (finite-type and
233	  geometric-irreducibility part); the verbatim source quote is
234	  reproduced at \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
235	  To establish geometric irreducibility and finite type, base-change
236	  to \(\overline k\) (which is permitted because formation of \(G^0\)
237	  commutes with field extension,
238	  \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}). Over
239	  \(\overline k\), the product
240	  \(G^0_{\red} \times G^0_{\red}\) is reduced (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.6.1), so
241	  \(G^0_{\red}\) is itself a subgroup; we may therefore replace \(G^0\) by
242	  \(G^0_{\red}\) for the geometric-irreducibility argument. Pick a
243	  nonempty smooth affine open subscheme \(U \subseteq G^0\) (such a \(U\)
244	  exists because \(G^0_{\red}\) is reduced and \(\overline k\) is
245	  algebraically closed). For any \(\overline k\)-point \(h \in G^0\) the
246	  translate \(hg^{-1} U\) (for \(g \in U(\overline k)\)) is a smooth open
247	  neighbourhood of \(h\), so \(G^0\) is locally irreducible at every closed
248	  point. Combined with connectedness, this gives irreducibility (EGA I
249	  6.1.10). The image \(\alpha(U \times U) = G^0\) is the surjective image
250	  of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme \(U \times U\), so \(G^0\) is
251	  quasi-compact; together with the locally-finite-type hypothesis on
252	  \(G\), this makes \(G^0\) of finite type over \(k\).
253	\end{proof}

281	  verbatim source quote is reproduced at
282	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
283	  Geometric connectedness of \(G^0\) follows from the existence of the
284	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in G^0(k)\): the identity component is
285	  connected and carries a \(k\)-rational section, so it is geometrically
286	  connected by
287	  \cref{lem:geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section} (the
288	  Stacks 037Q criterion; cf.\ EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14). In particular for
289	  any field extension \(K/k\) the base change \((G^0)_K\) is connected,
290	  contains the image of the identity section of \(G_K\), and is open and
291	  closed in \(G_K\) (since open- and closed-immersion both descend under
292	  base change, \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}). Hence
293	  \((G^0)_K\) coincides, as a subscheme of \(G_K\), with the identity
294	  component \((G_K)^0\) of \(G_K\); the natural comparison map
295	  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) is therefore an isomorphism.
296	\end{proof}

319	[Stacks 037Q iff-direction: connected + section ⟹ geometrically connected]
320	  \label{lem:geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section}
321	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section}
322	  % SOURCE: [Stacks], tag 037Q (criteria for geometric connectedness via
323	  % algebraic closure of the base field in the global sections) +
324	  % tag 04KV (geometrically connected descent of clopen partitions).
325	  % SOURCE QUOTE: Stacks 037Q (Lemma): "Let \(X\) be a scheme over the
326	  % field \(k\). Set \(K = H^0(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\). The following are
327	  % equivalent: (1) \(X\) is geometrically connected over \(k\), (2)
328	  % \(X\) is connected and \(k\) is algebraically closed in \(K\), (3)
329	  % \(K\) is a field and the inclusion \(k \subset K\) is purely
330	  % inseparable."  (read from references/stacks-varieties.tex section
331	  % \S geom-connected, tag 037Q).
332	  \textit{Source: Stacks, tag 037Q (iff-direction (2)\(\Rightarrow\)(1)).}
333	  Let \(k\) be a field and let \(f : X \to \Spec k\) be a morphism of
334	  schemes such that the underlying topological space \(|X|\) is
335	  connected. Suppose there is a \(k\)-rational section \(s : \Spec k \to X\)
336	  of \(f\) (so \(s \circ f = \id_X\) topologically and the image of \(s\)
337	  meets every irreducible component of \(X\) through which \(s\) factors).
338	  Then \(f : X \to \Spec k\) is geometrically connected, in the sense
339	  that for every field extension \(K/k\) the base change \(X_K\) is
340	  connected.
341	\end{lemma}
342	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Stacks 037Q proof, abridged: ``(2) ⟹ (1).  By
343	% Lemma 04KV, it suffices to show that \(X \times_k k'\) is connected
344	% for every finite separable field extension \(k'/k\).  By Lemma 0359
345	% \(X \times_k k' = \Spec(k') \times_{\Spec k} X\) and \(H^0(X
346	% \times_k k', \mathcal{O}) = K \otimes_k k'\).  Since \(k\) is
347	% algebraically closed in \(K\), the ring \(K \otimes_k k'\) is a
348	% field (a Henselian-type criterion); a scheme whose global section
349	% ring is a field is connected.''

351	  % DAG iter-266: removed spurious \uses{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
352	  % — the Stacks 037Q/04KV argument uses only connectedness of |X| and the
353	  % k-rational section, not the identity-component clopen theorem.
354	  Following Stacks 037Q (iff-direction (2)\(\Rightarrow\)(1)) combined
355	  with Stacks 04KV (geometrically connected descent of clopen partitions).
356	  By Stacks 04KV it suffices to prove \(X \times_k k'\) is connected
357	  for every finite separable extension \(k'/k\). The
358	  \(k\)-rational section \(s\) gives a \(k'\)-rational point of
359	  \(X \times_k k'\) (namely \(s \otimes \id\)), so \(X \times_k k'\) is
360	  nonempty. Moreover, since \(s\) is a section of \(f\), the image of
361	  \(s \otimes \id\) hits every clopen partition of \(X \times_k k'\)
362	  that has a \(k'\)-fibre — but if \(X \times_k k'\) admits a nontrivial
363	  clopen partition \(X \times_k k' = U \sqcup V\), the section
364	  \(s \otimes \id\) lies in exactly one of \(U, V\); pulling back along
365	  the natural map \(X \times_k k' \to X\) gives a corresponding clopen
366	  partition of \(|X|\) (the image of \(U\) and the image of \(V\)
367	  are disjoint clopens in \(|X|\) by surjectivity of \(\Spec k' \to \Spec
368	  k\)). Since \(|X|\) is connected by hypothesis, one of those images is
369	  empty, contradicting non-triviality. The base change \(X \times_k k'\)
370	  is therefore connected.

393	\(G = \Pic_{C/k}\).

395	\begin{definition}
396	[The identity component of the Picard scheme]
397	  \label{def:pic_zero_subscheme}
398	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme}
399	  
400	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, opening + Prp.~prp:pic0;
401	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
402	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2836-L2841 +
403	  % L2921-L2929).
404	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
405	  % "Having treated the existence of the Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\), we now
406	  % turn to its structure.  In this section, we study the union
407	  % \(\IPicz_{X/S}\) of the connected components of the identity element,
408	  % \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\), for \(s\in S\)."
409	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
410	  % "\begin{prp}\label{prp:pic0}
411	  %   Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\)
412	  % exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\).  Then \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is
413	  % separated, and it is smooth if it has a geometrically reduced open
414	  % subscheme.  Furthermore, the connected component of the identity
415	  % \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite type; it
416	  % is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with extending

420	  Prp.~prp:pic0.}
421	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field
422	  \(k\). The \emph{identity component of the Picard scheme}
423	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the identity component
424	  \((\Pic_{C/k})^0\) of the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) of
425	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}, in the sense of
426	  \cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}. By
427	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open
428	  and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) of finite type over \(k\),
429	  geometrically irreducible, and its formation commutes with
430	  extension of the base field.

433	\section{The degree map}
434	\label{sec:pic_degree}
435	
436	The disjoint-union structure of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
437	(\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union of open
438	quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) is indexed by the Hilbert polynomial
439	\(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) of a representing invertible sheaf
440	(\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}). For a smooth proper geometrically
441	integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g\) with a fixed degree-one polarisation
442	\(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\), the Hilbert polynomial of an invertible
443	sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is, by Riemann--Roch,
444	\(\Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n) = n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g\), so it is
445	linear in \(n\) with leading coefficient $\deg \mathcal{L} \in
446	\mathbb{Z}$. This identifies the connected components of
447	\(\Pic_{C/k}(\overline k)\) with the integers, the index being the
448	degree.
449	
450	\begin{definition}
451	[Degree of a Picard class]
452	  \label{def:divisor_degree_pic}
453	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
454	  \uses{def:pic_scheme, def:hilbert_polynomial, thm:fga_pic_representability}
455	
456	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
457	  % \S III.1, ``Definitions and main statements'', p.~88 (PDF p.~94)
458	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf).
459	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
460	  % "Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve over \(k\). The degree of a
461	  % divisor \(D = n_i P_i\) on \(C\) is \(n_i [k(P_i):k]\). Since every
462	  % invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is of the form \(\mathcal{L}(D)\)
463	  % for some divisor \(D\), and \(D\) is uniquely determined up to linear
464	  % equivalence, we can define \(\deg(\mathcal{L}) = \deg(D)\). Then
465	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L}^n) = \deg(n D) = n \deg(D)\), and the Riemann-Roch
466	  % theorem says that \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n) = n \deg(\mathcal{L}) + 1 - g\).
467	  % This gives a more canonical description of \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\): when
468	  % \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n)\) is written as a polynomial in \(n\),
469	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\) is the leading coefficient. We write
470	  % \(\Pic^0(C)\) for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves
471	  % of degree zero on \(C\)."
472	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
473	  \S III.1, p.~88.}
474	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
475	  \(g\), and let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
476	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}. The \emph{degree map}
477	  \[
478	    \deg \;\colon\; \Pic_{C/k}(k) \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}
479	  \]
480	  is defined as follows: a \(k\)-point \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\)
481	  represents (after \'etale base change) an invertible sheaf
482	  \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\); its Hilbert polynomial with respect to a fixed
483	  degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\) is, by
484	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial} and Riemann--Roch,
485	  \[
486	    \Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n)
487	    \;=\; \chi(C, \mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{O}_C(n))
488	    \;=\; n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g,
489	  \]
490	  a polynomial in \(n\) linear in \(n\). The integer
491	  \(\deg(\lambda) := \deg \mathcal{L}\) is the leading coefficient of
492	  this polynomial, well-defined on the isomorphism class
493	  \([\mathcal{L}]\) and (because \(\Pic_{C/k}\) represents the
494	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor) on the \(k\)-point
495	  \(\lambda\). The degree map is a group homomorphism: for
496	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\) invertible sheaves on \(C\) one has
497	  \(\deg(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}) = \deg \mathcal{L} + \deg \mathcal{M}\),
498	  because the Euler characteristic is additive on the additive groups of
499	  twists. Equivalently, the degree on \(k\)-points is the index of the
500	  connected component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing \(\lambda\) under the
501	  Hilbert-polynomial decomposition of
502	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: the disjoint union
503	  \(\Pic_{C/k} = \coprod_{d \in \mathbb{Z}} \Pic^d_{C/k}\) is indexed by
504	  the degree, with \(\Pic^d_{C/k}\) the component representing classes
505	  of degree \(d\).
506	\end{definition}

508	\section{The Pic-zero abelian variety}
509	\label{sec:pic0_abelian_variety}
510	
511	The terminal statement of the chapter identifies \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) with
512	an abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\).
513	Recall (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8) that a \(k\)-group scheme is an
514	\emph{abelian variety} iff it is a complete (proper) connected group
515	variety over \(k\); commutativity and projectivity then follow
516	automatically.

587	  We assemble the four conjuncts of the abelian-variety structure on
588	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) from four inputs.
589	
590	  (i) \emph{Clopen subgroup of finite type, geometrically irreducible.}
591	  By
592	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
593	  applied to \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite

597	  of finite type over \(k\), geometrically irreducible, and its
598	  formation commutes with extending \(k\). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
599	  is separated and locally of finite type as a subscheme of
600	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\), and connected.

603	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union
604	  of open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) restricted to \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)

607	  integral \(\Longrightarrow\) \(\Pic^0_{X/k}\) is quasi-projective).
608	
609	  (iii) \emph{Projectivity (properness).} For a smooth proper
610	  geometrically integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g > 0\), \(C/k\) is
611	  also geometrically normal (the smoothness implies geometric
612	  regularity, hence geometric normality). Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p
613	  then upgrades the quasi-projective conclusion to projective:
614	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is projective over \(k\), in particular proper.
615	  The proof of th:qpp\&p uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure
616	  theorem to reduce to ruling out non-constant maps
617	  \(\mathbb G_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), which on a geometrically normal
618	  \(X\) becomes a divisor-theoretic computation.
619	
620	  (iv) \emph{Smoothness.} For a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), the
621	  Picard scheme is smooth at \(0\): this is Exercise ex:jac of
622	  Kleiman~\S 5, which asserts that for \(X/S\) projective and flat
623	  with integral geometric fibres of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), the
624	  generalised Jacobians \(\Pic^0_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
625	  quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\); specialising
626	  to \(S = \Spec k\) and \(X = C\) a smooth proper curve of (geometric
627	  = arithmetic) genus \(g\) gives smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\).
628	
629	  Combining (i)--(iv): \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, projective,
630	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group scheme. A proper connected
631	  \(k\)-group variety is by definition an \emph{abelian variety}
632	  (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8), and is automatically commutative (Milne~\S I.1,
633	  Cor.~1.4). This establishes
634	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.

657	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
658	  \(g = g(C)\) (\cref{def:genus}) over a field \(k\). The dimension of
659	  the abelian variety \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
660	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}) equals the genus:
661	  \[
662	    \dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} \;=\; g(C).
663	  \]
664	\end{theorem}
665	
666	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
667	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3421-L3426,
668	% Cor.~cor:sm, dimension equals \(h^1(\mathcal O)\) when smooth:)
669	% "\begin{cor}\label{cor:sm} Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field
670	% \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Then
671	% \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
672	%  Equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then
673	% \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\)
674	% everywhere."
675	\begin{proof}
676	  \uses{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety, def:genus}
677	  By Kleiman~\S 5,
678	  Cor.~cor:sm, the inequality
679	  \(\dim \Pic_{C/k} \le \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\) holds, with
680	  equality if and only if \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\), in which
681	  case \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim_k H^1(C,
682	  \mathcal{O}_C)\) everywhere. The abelian-variety structure of
683	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety} (step (iv) of its proof)
684	  shows \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\); equality therefore holds
685	  on the open identity component, and
686	  \(\dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g(C)\)
687	  by \cref{def:genus}. This is Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e): ``The
688	  dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\)''.
689	\end{proof}
690	
691	\begin{theorem}
692	
693	\leanok
694	[\(k\)-points of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) are the kernel of the degree map]
695	  \label{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}
696	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.kPoints_iff_kerDegree}
697	  \uses{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety, thm:identity_component_open_subgroup, def:pic_zero_subscheme, def:divisor_degree_pic}
698	
699	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
700	  % \S III.1, p.~88; verbatim quote reproduced at
701	  % \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} (read from
702	  % references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF p.~94).
703	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
704	  \S III.1, p.~88 (``\(\Pic^0(C)\)\dots\ degree zero''); the verbatim
705	  source quote is at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}.}
706	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
707	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). A \(k\)-point
708	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the image of the inclusion of
709	  the identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
710	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} applied to
711	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), specialised in \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme})
712	  if and only if the degree
713	  \(\deg(\lambda) \in \mathbb{Z}\) of \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}
714	  vanishes. Equivalently,
715	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker\bigl(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z}\bigr)\).
716	\end{theorem}
717	
718	\begin{proof}
719	  \uses{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety, thm:identity_component_open_subgroup, def:pic_zero_subscheme, def:divisor_degree_pic}
720	  A class
721	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the connected component of
722	  the identity --- i.e.\ in the image of the open immersion
723	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
724	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}, specialised to
725	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) in \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) --- precisely when
726	  the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf coincides
727	  with that of \(\mathcal{O}_C\), equivalently when its degree (the
728	  leading coefficient of the Hilbert polynomial of
729	  \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is zero. Since \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the
730	  abelian variety of \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}, this image
731	  is exactly the degree-zero locus on \(k\)-points. Hence
732	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z})\),
733	  which is the content of Milne~\S III.1, p.~88 (the verbatim quote
734	  at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} explicitly writes ``\(\Pic^0(C)\)
735	  for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves of degree
736	  zero on \(C\)'').
737	\end{proof}

94	/-! ## §1. The identity component of a group scheme — abstract substrate
95	
96	The identity component is an abstract feature of `k`-group schemes locally of
97	finite type. We package the definition + open-and-closed-subgroup-scheme
98	structure in the `GroupScheme` namespace so they are reusable outside the
99	Picard context.
100	
101	In this project, a "`k`-group scheme" is encoded as an
102	`Over (Spec (.of k))`-object `G` carrying a `[GrpObj G]` instance (the
103	group-object structure in the over-category). The locally-of-finite-type
104	hypothesis is the `[LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom]` instance on the structural
105	morphism to the base.
106	
107	Blueprint references: `def:identity_component_group_scheme` +
108	`thm:identity_component_open_subgroup` (Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`). -/
109	
110	namespace GroupScheme
111	
112	/-- Helper (iter-188): in a **Noetherian** topological space `α`, the set of
113	connected components is finite.
114	
115	Proof: the map `irreducibleComponents α → ConnectedComponents α` sending each
116	irreducible component `C` to the connected component of an arbitrary chosen
117	point of `C` is well-defined (because `C` is preconnected, hence contained in
118	a single connected component) and surjective (because every point lies in
119	some irreducible component, namely `irreducibleComponent x`, which is a
120	subset of `connectedComponent x`). The source is finite by
121	`TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace.finite_irreducibleComponents`, so the
122	target is finite by `Finite.of_surjective`. -/
123	private lemma noetherianSpace_finite_connectedComponents
124	    {α : Type*} [TopologicalSpace α] [TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace α] :
125	    Finite (ConnectedComponents α) := by
126	  classical
127	  have hfin : (irreducibleComponents α).Finite :=
128	    TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace.finite_irreducibleComponents
129	  haveI : Finite ↥(irreducibleComponents α) := hfin.to_subtype
130	  refine Finite.of_surjective
131	    (fun C : ↥(irreducibleComponents α) =>
132	      (ConnectedComponents.mk : α → ConnectedComponents α)
133	        ((C.property : Maximal IsIrreducible C.val).prop.nonempty.some)) ?_
134	  intro c
135	  obtain ⟨x, rfl⟩ := ConnectedComponents.surjective_coe c
136	  refine ⟨⟨irreducibleComponent x, irreducibleComponent_mem_irreducibleComponents x⟩, ?_⟩
137	  rw [ConnectedComponents.coe_eq_coe]
138	  refine (connectedComponent_eq ?_).symm
139	  exact irreducibleComponent_subset_connectedComponent
140	    (isIrreducible_irreducibleComponent (x := x)).nonempty.some_mem
141	
142	/-- Helper (iter-188): in a **Noetherian** topological space `α`, each
143	connected component is open.
144	
145	Proof: `ConnectedComponents α` is totally disconnected
146	(`ConnectedComponents.totallyDisconnectedSpace`), hence T1
147	(`TotallyDisconnectedSpace.t1Space`), and finite by
148	`noetherianSpace_finite_connectedComponents`. A finite T1 space is discrete
149	(`Finite.instDiscreteTopology`), so the singleton
150	`{ConnectedComponents.mk x}` is open in the quotient. The preimage of this
151	singleton under the continuous quotient map `ConnectedComponents.mk` is
152	`connectedComponent x` (`connectedComponents_preimage_singleton`), which is
153	therefore open. -/
154	private lemma noetherianSpace_isOpen_connectedComponent
155	    {α : Type*} [TopologicalSpace α] [TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace α]
156	    (x : α) :

165	/-- Helper (iter-188): the **`LocallyConnectedSpace` instance** for the
166	underlying topological space of a `k`-scheme `G.left` whose structural
167	morphism `G.hom : G.left ⟶ Spec k` is locally of finite type.
168	
169	The substantive content is EGA I 6.1.9: a locally Noetherian topological
170	space has open connected components — equivalently, is locally connected.
171	Pushed through the chain: `Spec k` is Noetherian (a field has Noetherian
172	spectrum); `LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom + IsLocallyNoetherian (Spec k) ⟹
173	IsLocallyNoetherian G.left` (Mathlib's
174	`AlgebraicGeometry.LocallyOfFiniteType.isLocallyNoetherian`); and the
175	implication `IsLocallyNoetherian X ⟹ LocallyConnectedSpace X.toTopCat`
176	is the iter-188 project-side helper (`noetherianSpace_finite_connectedComponents`
177	+ `noetherianSpace_isOpen_connectedComponent`).

179	The classical proof: each point `y ∈ G.left` has an open affine
180	neighbourhood `W = Spec R` with `R` Noetherian, hence `|W|` is a Noetherian
181	topological space; in a Noetherian space, each `connectedComponent y` is
182	clopen (finite irreducible components ⟹ finite connected components ⟹
183	finite T1 quotient ⟹ discrete quotient ⟹ singletons clopen in quotient ⟹
184	preimages clopen). Pulled back along the open inclusion `W ↪ G.left`,
185	this gives an open connected neighbourhood of `y` inside any open `F`
186	containing `y`. -/
187	private instance identityComponent_locallyConnectedSpace
188	    {k : Type u} [Field k]
189	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
190	    [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
191	    LocallyConnectedSpace G.left := by
192	  haveI : IsLocallyNoetherian G.left := LocallyOfFiniteType.isLocallyNoetherian G.hom
193	  rw [locallyConnectedSpace_iff_subsets_isOpen_isConnected]
194	  intro x F hF
195	  -- Extract an open neighbourhood `T ⊆ F` of `x`.
196	  obtain ⟨T, hTF, hT, hxT⟩ := mem_nhds_iff.mp hF
197	  -- Find an affine open `W ⊆ T` with `x ∈ W`.
198	  obtain ⟨W, hW, hxW, hWT⟩ :=
199	    exists_isAffineOpen_mem_and_subset (X := G.left) (U := ⟨T, hT⟩) hxT
200	  haveI : IsNoetherianRing Γ(G.left, W) :=
201	    IsLocallyNoetherian.component_noetherian ⟨W, hW⟩
202	  haveI hNoethW : TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace ↥W :=
203	    noetherianSpace_of_isAffineOpen W hW
204	  -- Inside `W` (Noetherian) the connected component of `x` is open; its image
205	  -- under the open inclusion `W ↪ G.left` is the connected open neighbourhood
206	  -- we want.
207	  let xW : ↥W := ⟨x, hxW⟩
208	  refine ⟨(Subtype.val : ↥W → G.left) '' connectedComponent xW, ?_, ?_, ?_, ?_⟩

210	    exact hTF (hWT z.2)

212	      (noetherianSpace_isOpen_connectedComponent xW)
213	  · exact ⟨xW, mem_connectedComponent, rfl⟩
214	  · exact isConnected_connectedComponent.image _ continuous_subtype_val.continuousOn

259	/-- **The identity component is an open and closed subgroup scheme.**
260	
261	The bundled statement of Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`~(3): the identity
262	component `G^0 = IdentityComponent G` of a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of
263	finite type comes with a morphism `IdentityComponent G ⟶ G` (in
264	`Over (Spec (.of k))`) whose underlying scheme morphism is both an open
265	immersion and a closed immersion (i.e. the inclusion of a clopen
266	subscheme).
267	
268	The full Kleiman conclusion also packages the group-subscheme property (the
269	inclusion is a homomorphism of `k`-group schemes), finite-type-ness over `k`
270	(`LocallyOfFiniteType` + quasi-compactness), geometric irreducibility, and
271	base-change-commutation. Those refinements live as separate instances /
272	follow-up lemmas in iter-186+; the file-skeleton pins only the clopen
273	open-immersion conclusion as a Nonempty-witness.
274	
275	iter-186 body: the inclusion morphism is `Over.homMk (identityComponentCarrier G).ι`,
276	with the over-category compatibility holding by definition of
277	`IdentityComponent G`. The `.left` of this morphism is
278	`(identityComponentCarrier G).ι`, an open immersion by the global
279	`Scheme.Opens.instIsOpenImmersionι` instance. For the closed-immersion
280	half we apply `IsClosedImmersion.of_isPreimmersion` to the open immersion
281	and reduce to `IsClosed (↑(identityComponentCarrier G) : Set _)`. The
282	remaining inline `sorry` is the substantive content: the carrier of
283	`identityComponentCarrier G` is closed, by "closure of a connected subspace
284	is connected" applied to the connected component through the image of the
285	identity section. -/
286	theorem IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme {k : Type u} [Field k]
287	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
288	    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
289	    Nonempty {f : IdentityComponent G ⟶ G //
290	        IsOpenImmersion f.left ∧ IsClosedImmersion f.left} := by
291	  -- Over-category compatibility for the underlying open immersion.
292	  have hcomp : (identityComponentCarrier G).ι ≫ G.hom = (IdentityComponent G).hom := by
293	    simp [IdentityComponent]
294	  -- The over-morphism witness.
295	  let f : IdentityComponent G ⟶ G :=
296	    Over.homMk (U := IdentityComponent G) (V := G) (identityComponentCarrier G).ι hcomp
297	  refine ⟨⟨f, ?_, ?_⟩⟩
298	  · -- `f.left = (identityComponentCarrier G).ι` (definitionally; `Over.homMk_left` simp).
299	    -- An open immersion by `Scheme.Opens.instIsOpenImmersionι`.
300	    change IsOpenImmersion (identityComponentCarrier G).ι
301	    infer_instance
302	  · -- `f.left = (identityComponentCarrier G).ι` is a preimmersion (open immersion ⟹
303	    -- immersion ⟹ preimmersion). Its range is the carrier set (`Scheme.Opens.range_ι`),
304	    -- and closure of that connected subset is itself (Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`~(3):
305	    -- the connected component through the identity is closed in `|G|`).
306	    change IsClosedImmersion (identityComponentCarrier G).ι
307	    apply IsClosedImmersion.of_isPreimmersion
308	    rw [Scheme.Opens.range_ι]
309	    -- The carrier set is `connectedComponent (identitySectionPoint G)`, which is
310	    -- closed by `isClopen_connectedComponent.1` (with the
311	    -- `identityComponent_locallyConnectedSpace` instance providing the closedness
312	    -- half of EGA I 6.1.9: connected components of a locally Noetherian topological
313	    -- space are clopen).
314	    change IsClosed (connectedComponent (identitySectionPoint G))
315	    exact isClopen_connectedComponent.1
